solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int minChanges(int[] nums, int k) {
        int n = nums.length;
        int maxNum = 1 << 10;
        int[] dp = new int[maxNum];
        for (int i = 0; i < maxNum; i++) dp[i] = i;

        for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
            int[] cnt = new int[maxNum];
            for (int j = i; j < n; j += k) {
                cnt[nums[j]]++;
            }
            int[] newDp = new int[maxNum];
            int minChanges = n;
            for (int x = 0; x < maxNum; x++) {
                for (int y = 0; y < maxNum; y++) {
                    int z = x ^ y;
                    minChanges = Math.min(minChanges, dp[z] + cnt[y]);
                }
            }
            for (int x = 0; x < maxNum; x++) {
                dp[x] = minChanges;
            }
        }

        return dp[0];
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n * 2^m), where m is the number of bits in the maximum number (10 in this case)
space complexity: O(2^m), where m is the number of bits in the maximum number (10 in this case)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses dynamic programming to track the minimum number of changes needed to make all segments of size k have XOR equal to zero. It iterates over each position in the first k elements, counts the frequency of each number in the corresponding positions, and computes the minimum changes needed for each possible XOR value. The key strength is its efficient use of dynamic programming and bitwise operations, while the main weakness is the exponential dependency on the number of bits in the maximum number.
